content.text: FastAPI's built-in OpenAPI integration and its deployment characteristics are not separate concerns — the same type annotations that drive runtime validation also generate the API contract, and the same async architecture that enables high concurrency determines how the application is correctly packaged and scaled for production.

(formerly Swagger) is a language-agnostic specification for describing REST APIs. FastAPI generates a fully compliant OpenAPI 3.x schema automatically from route decorators, Pydantic models, path/query/header parameters, and response models — with zero additional configuration required. This schema powers the interactive documentation UIs (
) and serves as the machine-readable contract for client generation, API gateways, and integration testing.

covers everything required to run a FastAPI application reliably in production: choosing and configuring ASGI servers, containerization, process management, reverse proxy configuration, environment management, health checking, observability, and horizontal scaling strategies.

Together, OpenAPI and deployment close the loop between development and production. The OpenAPI schema generated during development becomes the contract enforced by API gateways and consumed by client SDKs in production. The deployment architecture determines whether the async concurrency advantages of FastAPI are actually realized under load, or whether they are negated by process-per-request models, synchronous drivers, or misconfigured connection pools.
Neglecting either produces specific failure modes:
Missing or inaccurate OpenAPI schemas break auto-generated clients and API gateway routing rules
Deploying FastAPI behind a WSGI server (like a bare Gunicorn without a UvicornWorker) silently drops all async benefits
Running a single Uvicorn process without Gunicorn loses automatic worker restart on crashes
Missing health check endpoints cause Kubernetes to route traffic to unhealthy pods
FastAPI constructs the OpenAPI schema by inspecting every registered route at startup:
with types and constraints → parameter schemas

from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()

# Schema is available at runtime
schema = app.openapi()   # Returns the full OpenAPI dict

from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI(
    title="My SaaS API",
    summary="Platform API for managing users, orders, and billing",
    description="""
## Overview
This API provides programmatic access to all platform features.

## Authentication
All endpoints (except `/auth/token` and `/health`) require a Bearer token.

## Rate Limiting
Requests are limited to 100 per minute per authenticated user.
    """,
    version="2.1.0",
    terms_of_service="https://example.com/terms",
    contact={
        "name": "Platform Team",
        "url": "https://example.com/support",
        "email": "api@example.com",
    },
    license_info={
        "name": "Apache 2.0",
        "url": "https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0.html",
    },
    openapi_url="/api/openapi.json",   # Change schema URL
    docs_url="/api/docs",              # Change Swagger UI URL
    redoc_url="/api/redoc",            # Change ReDoc URL
)
field renders Markdown in both Swagger UI and ReDoc.

from fastapi import APIRouter

router = APIRouter()

@router.post(
    "/users/",
    response_model=UserResponse,
    status_code=201,
    summary="Create a new user account",
    description="""
Creates a new user account and sends a welcome email.

The password is hashed before storage and is never returned.
Usernames must be unique and may only contain alphanumeric characters and underscores.
    """,
    response_description="The created user object",
    tags=["Users"],
    deprecated=False,
    operation_id="create_user",   # Overrides auto-generated operationId
    include_in_schema=True,       # Set False to hide from docs
)
async def create_user(user: UserCreate) -> UserResponse:
    ...

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, ConfigDict
from typing import Optional

class UserCreate(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(
        json_schema_extra={
            "example": {
                "username": "alice_smith",
                "email": "alice@example.com",
                "password": "securepassword123"
            }
        }
    )

    username: str = Field(
        ...,
        min_length=3,
        max_length=50,
        pattern=r"^[a-zA-Z0-9_]+$",
        description="Unique username. Only alphanumeric characters and underscores.",
        examples=["alice_smith", "bob_jones_42"]
    )
    email: str = Field(
        ...,
        description="Valid email address used for login and notifications",
        examples=["alice@example.com"]
    )
    password: str = Field(
        ...,
        min_length=8,
        description="Password (min 8 characters). Stored hashed, never returned.",
        examples=["securepassword123"]
    )

class OrderCreate(BaseModel):
    """
    Request body for creating a new order.

    All products must be active and in sufficient stock.
    The total is calculated server-side from current prices.
    """
    items: list[OrderItemCreate] = Field(
        ...,
        min_length=1,
        description="List of products and quantities to order"
    )
    shipping_address_id: Optional[int] = Field(
        default=None,
        description="ID of a saved shipping address. If omitted, uses account default."
    )
    coupon_code: Optional[str] = Field(
        default=None,
        max_length=50,
        description="Optional promotional coupon code",
        examples=["SUMMER20", "NEWUSER10"]
    )

from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.openapi.utils import get_openapi

def custom_openapi():
    if app.openapi_schema:
        return app.openapi_schema

    schema = get_openapi(
        title=app.title,
        version=app.version,
        description=app.description,
        routes=app.routes,
    )

    # Add security scheme definitions
    schema["components"]["securitySchemes"] = {
        "BearerAuth": {
            "type": "http",
            "scheme": "bearer",
            "bearerFormat": "JWT",
            "description": "JWT token obtained from POST /auth/token"
        },
        "ApiKeyAuth": {
            "type": "apiKey",
            "in": "header",
            "name": "X-API-Key",
            "description": "API key for server-to-server communication"
        }
    }

    # Apply security globally (can be overridden per-route)
    schema["security"] = [{"BearerAuth": []}, {"ApiKeyAuth": []}]

    # Add server environments
    schema["servers"] = [
        {"url": "https://api.example.com/v2", "description": "Production"},
        {"url": "https://staging-api.example.com/v2", "description": "Staging"},
        {"url": "http://localhost:8000", "description": "Local development"},
    ]

    app.openapi_schema = schema
    return app.openapi_schema

app.openapi = custom_openapi

# Hide internal or webhook endpoints from public docs
@app.post("/internal/sync/", include_in_schema=False)
async def internal_sync():
    ...

@app.post("/webhooks/stripe/", include_in_schema=False)
async def stripe_webhook():
    ...

# Disable docs entirely in production
app = FastAPI(
    docs_url=None,      # Disables Swagger UI
    redoc_url=None,     # Disables ReDoc
    openapi_url=None,   # Disables schema endpoint entirely
)
Disabling docs and the schema endpoint in production reduces information leakage and removes an unauthenticated surface from the attack surface.
To keep docs accessible in production but gated behind credentials:
The OpenAPI schema enables automatic client generation in any language. This is one of the primary productivity benefits of maintaining accurate schemas.

# Install openapi-generator
npm install -g @openapitools/openapi-generator-cli

# Generate Python client
openapi-generator-cli generate \
  -i https://api.example.com/openapi.json \
  -g python \
  -o ./clients/python \
  --additional-properties=packageName=my_api_client

# Generate TypeScript/Axios client
openapi-generator-cli generate \
  -i https://api.example.com/openapi.json \
  -g typescript-axios \
  -o ./clients/typescript

# Generate Go client
openapi-generator-cli generate \
  -i https://api.example.com/openapi.json \
  -g go \
  -o ./clients/go
— generates modern Python clients with Pydantic models
— generates SDKs with idiomatic patterns per language
— generates production-quality SDKs with retry logic built in
FastAPI is an ASGI application. It requires an ASGI server to run. The choice of server and its configuration directly affects performance, reliability, and operational characteristics.
Uvicorn is the standard ASGI server for FastAPI. It implements the ASGI interface using
for higher performance) and handles HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2 (with
pip install uvicorn[standard]   # Includes uvloop, httptools, websockets
watches for file changes and restarts the server. Never use in production — it consumes additional resources and introduces restart latency.

For production deployments requiring process-level redundancy, Gunicorn manages multiple Uvicorn worker processes. If one worker crashes, Gunicorn restarts it automatically without taking the application offline.

pip install gunicorn
gunicorn main:app \
  --workers 4 \
  --worker-class uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker \
  --bind 0.0.0.0:8000 \
  --timeout 120 \
  --keepalive 5 \
  --max-requests 1000 \
  --max-requests-jitter 100 \
  --graceful-timeout 30 \
  --log-level warning \
  --access-logfile - \
  --error-logfile -
Number of worker processes (typically 2–4 × CPU cores)
Kill worker if it doesn't respond within N seconds
Restart workers after N requests (prevents memory leaks)
Random offset to prevent all workers restarting simultaneously
Grace period for in-flight requests during shutdown
The worker count rule of thumb:
for I/O-bound applications (typical for FastAPI). For a 2-core machine, 5 workers. Monitor CPU and memory under load and adjust.
Worker count does not increase async concurrency within a worker
— each Uvicorn worker already handles thousands of concurrent requests via asyncio. More workers increases throughput by utilizing additional CPU cores, not by adding async capacity.

# Multi-stage build — keeps final image small
FROM python:3.12-slim AS builder

WORKDIR /build

# Install build dependencies
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    gcc \
    libpq-dev \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Install Python dependencies into a virtual environment
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN python -m venv /opt/venv && \
    /opt/venv/bin/pip install --no-cache-dir --upgrade pip && \
    /opt/venv/bin/pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Final image — no build tools
FROM python:3.12-slim AS runtime

# Security: run as non-root
RUN useradd --create-home --shell /bin/bash appuser

WORKDIR /app

# Install only runtime system dependencies
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    libpq5 \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Copy virtual environment from builder stage
COPY --from=builder /opt/venv /opt/venv

# Copy application code
COPY --chown=appuser:appuser . .

# Use virtual environment
ENV PATH="/opt/venv/bin:$PATH"
ENV PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1
ENV PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1

# Security: drop to non-root user
USER appuser

EXPOSE 8000

# Use exec form (not shell form) to ensure proper signal handling
CMD ["gunicorn", "main:app", \
     "--workers", "4", \
     "--worker-class", "uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker", \
     "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000", \
     "--timeout", "120", \
     "--max-requests", "1000", \
     "--max-requests-jitter", "100", \
     "--graceful-timeout", "30", \
     "--log-level", "warning", \
     "--access-logfile", "-", \
     "--error-logfile", "-"]

# docker-compose.yml
version: "3.9"

services:
  api:
    build:
      context: .
      target: runtime
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    environment:
      - DATABASE_URL=postgresql+asyncpg://user:password@postgres:5432/mydb
      - REDIS_URL=redis://redis:6379
      - SECRET_KEY=${SECRET_KEY}
      - ENVIRONMENT=development
    volumes:
      - .:/app   # Hot reload in development
    command: uvicorn main:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
      redis:
        condition: service_started

  postgres:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: user
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: password
      POSTGRES_DB: mydb
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U user -d mydb"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 5

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    volumes:
      - redis_data:/data

  celery_worker:
    build:
      context: .
      target: runtime
    command: celery -A tasks.celery_app worker --concurrency=4 --loglevel=info
    environment:
      - DATABASE_URL=postgresql+asyncpg://user:password@postgres:5432/mydb
      - REDIS_URL=redis://redis:6379
    depends_on:
      - postgres
      - redis

volumes:
  postgres_data:
  redis_data:

In production, FastAPI/Gunicorn sits behind a reverse proxy. nginx handles TLS termination, static file serving, request buffering, and connection management, forwarding clean HTTP to the application.
headers must be trusted by FastAPI — add Starlette's
returns the proxy IP rather than the real client IP, breaking rate limiting and logging.
Health check endpoints are required by Kubernetes, load balancers, and container orchestrators to determine whether to route traffic to an instance.

from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession
import redis.asyncio as aioredis
import time

@app.get("/health", include_in_schema=False)
async def health_check():
    """
    Basic liveness probe — returns 200 if the application process is running.
    Does not check downstream dependencies.
    """
    return {"status": "healthy", "timestamp": time.time()}

@app.get("/health/ready", include_in_schema=False)
async def readiness_check(request: Request):
    """
    Readiness probe — returns 200 only if all dependencies are reachable.
    Kubernetes uses this to decide whether to send traffic.
    """
    checks = {}
    overall_healthy = True

    # Database check
    try:
        async with AsyncSessionLocal() as session:
            await session.execute(text("SELECT 1"))
        checks["database"] = "healthy"
    except Exception as exc:
        checks["database"] = f"unhealthy: {str(exc)}"
        overall_healthy = False

    # Redis check
    try:
        redis = request.app.state.redis
        await redis.ping()
        checks["redis"] = "healthy"
    except Exception as exc:
        checks["redis"] = f"unhealthy: {str(exc)}"
        overall_healthy = False

    status_code = 200 if overall_healthy else 503
    return JSONResponse(
        status_code=status_code,
        content={
            "status": "ready" if overall_healthy else "not_ready",
            "checks": checks,
            "timestamp": time.time()
        }
    )

@app.get("/health/live", include_in_schema=False)
async def liveness_check():
    """
    Liveness probe — Kubernetes kills and restarts the pod if this returns non-200.
    Should only check if the process itself is functioning, not dependencies.
    """
    return {"status": "alive"}

# kubernetes/deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: fastapi-app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: api
          image: myapp:latest
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /health/live
              port: 8000
            initialDelaySeconds: 10
            periodSeconds: 30
            failureThreshold: 3
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /health/ready
              port: 8000
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
            periodSeconds: 10
            failureThreshold: 3
          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "256Mi"
              cpu: "250m"
            limits:
              memory: "512Mi"
              cpu: "1000m"
          env:
            - name: DATABASE_URL
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: app-secrets
                  key: database-url
            - name: SECRET_KEY
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: app-secrets
                  key: secret-key

# .env.example (committed to version control)
DATABASE_URL=postgresql+asyncpg://user:password@localhost:5432/mydb
SECRET_KEY=change-me-to-at-least-32-random-characters
REDIS_URL=redis://localhost:6379
ENVIRONMENT=development
DEBUG=true

# .env (NOT committed — listed in .gitignore)
DATABASE_URL=postgresql+asyncpg://prod_user:prod_password@prod-db.internal:5432/proddb
SECRET_KEY=actual-production-secret-key-from-secrets-manager
REDIS_URL=redis://prod-redis.internal:6379
ENVIRONMENT=production
DEBUG=false
For production, use a secrets manager rather than
context manager handles startup and shutdown events — initializing connection pools, registering background workers, and cleaning up resources gracefully:

from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from fastapi import FastAPI
import redis.asyncio as aioredis

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    # ── Startup ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    from database import engine, AsyncSessionLocal
    from sqlalchemy import text

    # Verify database connectivity at startup
    try:
        async with AsyncSessionLocal() as session:
            await session.execute(text("SELECT 1"))
    except Exception as exc:
        raise RuntimeError(f"Database connection failed at startup: {exc}")

    # Initialize Redis connection pool
    app.state.redis = await aioredis.from_url(
        settings.redis_url,
        encoding="utf-8",
        decode_responses=True,
        max_connections=20
    )

    # Initialize HTTP client for external API calls
    import httpx
    app.state.http_client = httpx.AsyncClient(
        timeout=30.0,
        limits=httpx.Limits(max_keepalive_connections=20, max_connections=100)
    )

    yield   # Application runs

    # ── Shutdown ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    await app.state.redis.close()
    await app.state.http_client.aclose()
    await engine.dispose()

app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)
gives in-flight requests time to complete before forcibly stopping workers. The lifespan
), ensuring resources are always released even if startup partially fails.

Internet → nginx (TLS) → Gunicorn + 4 × UvicornWorker → PostgreSQL
                                                         → Redis
Suitable for moderate traffic. All components on one server. nginx handles TLS and buffering; Gunicorn manages worker restarts.

Internet
    │
    ▼
Cloud Load Balancer (TLS termination)
    │
    ├──► API Pod 1: Gunicorn + UvicornWorkers
    ├──► API Pod 2: Gunicorn + UvicornWorkers
    └──► API Pod 3: Gunicorn + UvicornWorkers
                │
                ├──► PostgreSQL (RDS / Cloud SQL)
                ├──► Redis (ElastiCache / Memorystore)
                └──► Object Storage (S3 / GCS)
Each API pod is stateless — no local state that isn't in the database or Redis. The load balancer distributes connections; sticky sessions are only needed for WebSocket connections.

# kubernetes/hpa.yaml
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: fastapi-app-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: fastapi-app
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 20
  metrics:
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: cpu
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 70
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: memory
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 80

from prometheus_fastapi_instrumentator import Instrumentator

Instrumentator(
    should_group_status_codes=True,
    should_ignore_untemplated=True,
    should_respect_env_var=True,
    excluded_handlers=["/health", "/metrics"],
).instrument(app).expose(app, include_in_schema=False)
endpoint with request count, latency histograms, and response code distributions per endpoint — consumable by Prometheus and Grafana.

from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.instrumentation.fastapi import FastAPIInstrumentor
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter

provider = TracerProvider()
provider.add_span_processor(
    BatchSpanProcessor(OTLPSpanExporter(endpoint="http://otel-collector:4317"))
)
trace.set_tracer_provider(provider)

FastAPIInstrumentor.instrument_app(app)
This automatically traces every request with spans for routing, middleware, and dependency resolution, exportable to Jaeger, Zipkin, or any OTLP-compatible backend.

# .github/workflows/deploy.yaml
name: Test and Deploy

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16
        env:
          POSTGRES_USER: test_user
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: test_password
          POSTGRES_DB: test_db
        options: >-
          --health-cmd pg_isready
          --health-interval 5s
          --health-timeout 3s
          --health-retries 5
      redis:
        image: redis:7
        options: --health-cmd "redis-cli ping"

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"
          cache: pip

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install -r requirements.txt -r requirements-dev.txt

      - name: Run linting
        run: |
          ruff check .
          mypy .

      - name: Run migrations
        run: alembic upgrade head
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: postgresql://test_user:test_password@localhost:5432/test_db

      - name: Run tests
        run: pytest --cov=app --cov-report=xml -x
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: postgresql+asyncpg://test_user:test_password@localhost:5432/test_db
          SECRET_KEY: test-secret-key-32-chars-minimum!!
          ENVIRONMENT: testing

      - name: Upload coverage
        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v4

  deploy:
    needs: test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Build Docker image
        run: docker build -t myapp:${{ github.sha }} .

      - name: Push to registry
        run: |
          echo ${{ secrets.REGISTRY_PASSWORD }} | docker login -u ${{ secrets.REGISTRY_USER }} --password-stdin
          docker push myapp:${{ github.sha }}

      - name: Run database migrations
        run: |
          # Run Alembic before updating the deployment
          kubectl run alembic-migrate --image=myapp:${{ github.sha }} \
            --restart=Never \
            --env="DATABASE_URL=${{ secrets.DATABASE_URL }}" \
            -- alembic upgrade head

      - name: Deploy to Kubernetes
        run: |
          kubectl set image deployment/fastapi-app api=myapp:${{ github.sha }}
          kubectl rollout status deployment/fastapi-app --timeout=300s
1. Running FastAPI with a WSGI server

# WRONG — gunicorn alone uses a sync WSGI worker; FastAPI is ASGI
gunicorn main:app

# CORRECT — use UvicornWorker to get ASGI support
gunicorn main:app --worker-class uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker
causes FastAPI to appear to work (Starlette has WSGI compatibility) but all async concurrency benefits are lost.
spawns a file watcher process, increases memory usage, and restarts workers on any file change. It is a development-only flag.

# WRONG for I/O-bound FastAPI — too many workers starve the DB connection pool
gunicorn main:app --workers 32 --worker-class uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker
Each worker holds its own connection pool. 32 workers × 10 connections = 320 database connections. Most databases have limits around 100–200. Start with
and adjust based on measured throughput and connection pool utilization.
4. Exposing OpenAPI schema in production without protection
The OpenAPI schema documents every endpoint, parameter, authentication mechanism, and error response — a detailed map for attackers. Either disable it (
, rate limiting uses the proxy's IP instead of the client's IP — every client looks the same, and rate limits are either never triggered or trigger for all clients simultaneously.

# WRONG — multiple pods running this simultaneously corrupt the migration state
@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app):
    await run_alembic_migrations()   # Race condition with other pods
    yield
Migrations must run as a separate pre-deployment job, not at startup.
Without graceful shutdown, Gunicorn immediately kills workers on SIGTERM, dropping all in-flight requests.
on all Pydantic fields — it produces documentation that clients actually understand without referring back to developers.
unless explicitly needed — or protect them with authentication. The schema is detailed attack surface documentation.
on frequently used endpoints — auto-generated IDs (like
) produce ugly client SDK method names.
Run Alembic in a pre-deployment job
, not lifespan — this prevents race conditions in multi-pod deployments and surfaces migration failures before traffic shifts to the new version.
whenever deployed behind nginx or a cloud load balancer — without it,
Expose separate liveness and readiness endpoints
— liveness checks only whether the process is alive; readiness checks whether dependencies are reachable. Kubernetes uses them differently.
to periodically restart Gunicorn workers — it prevents gradual memory growth from leaking over time.
— log aggregation systems (Datadog, CloudWatch, Loki) parse JSON natively, enabling filtering by request ID, user ID, or status code without regex.
Add Prometheus metrics from day one
— request rate, error rate, and latency per endpoint are the minimum signals needed to understand production behavior.
changes silently and breaks reproducible builds.
FastAPI generates OpenAPI 3.x schemas automatically from type annotations — enriching Pydantic models with
produces documentation that is accurate by construction.
The OpenAPI schema is a production artifact with security implications — it should be disabled or protected behind authentication in production environments.
FastAPI requires an ASGI server — Uvicorn for development and single-process deployments, Gunicorn with
for production multi-process deployments. Running with a bare WSGI Gunicorn worker silently eliminates all async concurrency benefits.

Worker count does not increase async concurrency within a single process — each Uvicorn worker already handles thousands of concurrent connections via asyncio. More workers utilize more CPU cores and provide process-level redundancy.
The liveness/readiness probe split is architecturally significant — liveness detects a broken process (restart it), readiness detects unavailable dependencies (stop sending traffic, don't restart).
Database migrations must run as a pre-deployment job, not at application startup — startup migrations race across multiple pods and fail at the worst possible time (during a traffic-live deployment).

Structured JSON logging, Prometheus metrics, and distributed tracing are not production luxuries — they are the minimum observability infrastructure needed to understand and debug a running FastAPI application.
Connection pool sizing is the most common production performance mistake — each Gunicorn worker holds its own pool, so
must not exceed the database's connection limit.
